ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts; closest match is pork rib soup (FOODON_03311737). Peanut component not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a pork-rib-dominant mixed soup dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts. A0CKP (cooked pork) used as the primary protein anchor. Herbal broth and peanut components are not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bak Kut Teh with peanuts is approximately 400 g, comprising pork ribs, softened peanuts, and herbal broth.

nutrient_source.title: IMR Malaysia MyFCD — Bak Kut Teh / Pork Rib Soup with Peanuts; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for peanut component cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single IMR MyFCD entry exactly matches Bak Kut Teh with peanuts as a combined dish. Values are adapted from the IMR MyFCD pork rib soup entry and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy peanut data (NDB 16087) and HPB Singapore Foodo Bak Kut Teh reference. The input values (energy 112.5 kcal, protein 8.75 g, fat 7.0 g, carbohydrate 3.75 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Sodium 300 mg/100 g is consistent with a soy-sauce-seasoned herbal broth.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Peanuts GI = 14 (boiled); analogous estimate applied to peanut-dominant low-carbohydrate soup dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts. GI estimated at 25 based on: (1) peanuts have a published GI of 14 (boiled) per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) pork ribs and herbal broth contribute negligible carbohydrate; (3) the dish carbohydrate content is 3.75 g/100 g, predominantly from peanuts. A conservative upward adjustment to GI 25 accounts for the small residual carbohydrate from soy sauce and trace starch in the broth. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (25 × 15 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.75, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and salt in herbal broth; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced or low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid drinking the braising liquid to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 3.75 g per 100 g and sugar 1.0 g per 100 g are very low; peanuts have a published GI of 14 and the estimated dish GI of 25 is well within the low range; protein 8.75 g per 100 g supports glycaemic modulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bak Kut Teh with peanuts is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index; however, portion size should be moderated to limit caloric intake from fat, and excess braising liquid should be avoided to minimise sodium load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.5 g per 100 g from pork ribs and cholesterol 25 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers 10 g saturated fat (approximately 50% of the 20 g daily limit) and 100 mg cholesterol; sodium 1200 mg per serving also elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions, choose leaner cuts or request less pork rib, avoid drinking the broth, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs are red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise pork rib consumption in this dish; a peanut-only or tofu-substituted version of the herbal broth is preferable, as peanuts and the herbal spices (cinnamon, cloves, garlic) provide some anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh with Peanuts (肉骨茶花生) is a Malaysian-Singaporean herbal pork rib soup in which tender pork ribs and softened peanuts are slow-simmered in a fragrant broth seasoned with garlic, cinnamon, cloves, star anise, soy sauce, and salt. The dish originates from the Hokkien and Teochew communities of Malaysia and Singapore and is traditionally eaten as a breakfast or lunch meal, often accompanied by rice, youtiao (fried dough fritters), and dark soy sauce for dipping. The broth is deeply savoury, slightly herbal, and mildly spiced; the pork ribs become fall-off-the-bone tender after prolonged simmering, while the peanuts absorb the broth and develop a creamy, earthy texture. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 112.5 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 7.0 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and only 3.75 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is notable at 300 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and salt in the broth. The dish has a very low glycaemic index (estimated GI 25) due to the peanut component and negligible starch content. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The pork rib component is pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. The dish is not halal due to pork and contains peanut and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides an exact nutrient profile for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts as a combined dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD pork rib soup data cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy peanut data and HPB Singapore Foodo Bak Kut Teh reference. The input values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on published peanut GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Bak Kut Teh with peanuts as a composite dish is not available in IMR MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in IMR MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace — pork ribs and peanuts in a cooked herbal broth contain effectively no vitamin C after prolonged simmering; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork and boiled peanuts. trans_fat_g is 0.03: trace level from pork fat, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork ribs. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Allergens: peanuts and soy (from soy sauce). The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but the primary nutrient evidence is attributed to IMR MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources; the grabfood/foodo.sg provenance from the input is not used as primary nutrient evidence.
